1.synthesis: Paul recalls how he came to Corinth: "not with lofty speech or wisdom," but resolved "to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and him crucified" (vv.1–2). He was "in weakness and in fear and much trembling," and his message came "not in plausible words of wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power" (vv.3–4), so that their faith "might not rest in the wisdom of men but in the power of God" (v.5). Paul's own ministry embodied the cross he preached: weakness as the vehicle of God's power.
1.historical_context: Paul came to Corinth from Athens, where his philosophical address at the Areopagus met a largely unresponsive audience (Acts 17:32–34); his deliberate turn to plain proclamation may reflect that experience. "Lofty speech" (v.1) refers to Sophistic rhetoric, in which traveling speakers competed for fees and prestige by displays of eloquence — a competition Paul pointedly declined. His self-description "in weakness and in fear and much trembling" (v.3) is the opposite of the Sophist's confident authority, a manner shaped by the crucified Christ he proclaimed. The "demonstration of the Spirit and power" (v.4) — likely transformed lives and accompanying signs — replaces philosophical "proof" (apodeixis) with Spirit-wrought evidence.
1.literary_structure: Having argued that the cross confounds human wisdom (1 Corinthians 1:18–31), Paul now shows that his own preaching matched that message in form as well as content: a cross-shaped messenger for a cross-shaped gospel. "Jesus Christ and him crucified" (v.2) is programmatic for the entire letter — every later issue is judged from the cross. The contrast between "plausible words of wisdom" and "demonstration of the Spirit and power" (v.4) restates the wisdom-versus-power antithesis and prepares for the pivot in v.6 to the true wisdom God reveals by his Spirit.
1.theological_application: The integrity of gospel ministry lies in letting the message, not the messenger, carry the power: Paul deliberately set aside impressive technique so that faith would rest "in the power of God" rather than human eloquence (v.5). A faith built on a preacher's charisma is fragile, collapsing before a more eloquent rival — exactly the Corinthian factionalism. There is freedom here for the timid: Paul ministered "in weakness and… trembling" (v.3) and God worked powerfully, so effectiveness depends on the Spirit, not on the servant's strength or polish.
1.christology: Paul's single theme — "Jesus Christ and him crucified" (v.2) — makes the crucified Christ the sum and center of all Christian knowing. His own weakness in proclaiming it reflects the cross's own logic: as Christ "was crucified in weakness" yet "lives by the power of God" (cf. 2 Corinthians 13:4), so the apostle's frailty becomes the channel of God's power. The "demonstration of the Spirit and power" (v.4) is the risen Christ actively authenticating the message of his own cross.

6.synthesis: Lest he seem to despise wisdom altogether, Paul clarifies: "among the mature we do impart wisdom," but "a secret and hidden wisdom of God," decreed "before the ages for our glory," which "the rulers of this age" did not understand, "for had they understood it, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory" (vv.6–8). What "no eye has seen, nor ear heard" God has revealed "through the Spirit," who "searches… the depths of God" (vv.9–10). The natural person cannot receive the things of the Spirit, but "the spiritual person judges all things" (vv.14–15); and "we have the mind of Christ" (v.16). True wisdom is not human attainment but divine revelation.
6.historical_context: "The mature" (teleioi, v.6) and "mystery" (mystērion, v.7) echo the vocabulary of the mystery religions and Hellenistic philosophy, but Paul redefines them by Jewish apocalyptic: the "mystery" is God's saving plan, once hidden and now disclosed by the Spirit (cf. Daniel 2:28), not a secret reserved for initiates. "The rulers of this age" (v.8) likely names both the human authorities who crucified Jesus and the cosmic powers behind them (cf. Colossians 2:15). The citation of Isaiah 64:4 (v.9) underscores that the gospel's realities exceed natural knowledge, and the psychikos/pneumatikos contrast (vv.14–15) marks the difference not of education but of whether one has received the Spirit.
6.literary_structure: This completes the wisdom argument of chapters 1–2 by distinguishing the false "wisdom of this age" from the true "wisdom of God" (vv.6–7), reframing the whole debate epistemologically: the issue is not intelligence but the Spirit. Paul builds a contrast between two modes of knowing — the natural person who "cannot understand" spiritual things and the spiritual person taught by the Spirit (vv.14–15). The climactic citation of Isaiah 40:13 — "who has understood the mind of the Lord?" — is answered with the astonishing claim "we have the mind of Christ" (v.16), which grounds apostolic teaching while keeping it entirely a gift.
6.theological_application: Spiritual truth is spiritually discerned: "the natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit of God… and he is not able to understand them" (v.14), so understanding the gospel is a work of the Spirit, not merely of intellect. This humbles the clever and encourages the simple — the deepest wisdom is open to any Spirit-indwelt believer, not the preserve of an educated elite. That "we have the mind of Christ" (v.16) is not a claim to omniscience but to Spirit-given participation in Christ's own perspective, a gift to be received humbly and used to discern "all things" by the cross.
6.christology: The chilling note that the rulers of this age "crucified the Lord of glory" (v.8) joins the deepest humiliation to the highest dignity: the One nailed to a cross is "the Lord of glory," a divine title (cf. Psalms 24:7–10). The hidden wisdom of God, planned "before the ages" (v.7), is the saving purpose centered in this crucified Lord. And to "have the mind of Christ" (v.16) is to share, by the Spirit, the perspective of the One who is himself the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24).
